title: June 2026 - Liberty Street Economics
gist: The NY Fed Liberty Street Economics June 2026 index covers four research findings: remote work explains 64% of the rise in youth unemployment (not generative AI); interest rate caps reallocate credit away from risky borrowers without improving their delinquencies; regional small businesses in the NY metro area are severely pessimistic about 2026; and synthetic stablecoins transmitted and amplified the Oct 10 tariff shock across crypto markets.

themes.theme: Remote Work, Rate Caps, and Regional Economy

insights.claim: NY Fed research finds that remote work can explain 64% of the recent increase in unemployment among young college graduates—and that remote work, not generative AI, explains the bulk of the rise in youth unemployment, because remote arrangements make it harder for managers to train and mentor new employees.
insights.speaker: Natalia Emanuel, Emma Harrington, Amanda Pallais
insights.so_what: The AI-is-displacing-youth narrative is overstated; the dominant driver of youth unemployment is structural (remote work + training friction), not automation—repositioning the policy and investment debate toward office/hybrid work design rather than AI job displacement.
insights.quote: We estimate that remote work can explain 64 percent of the recent increase in unemployment among young college graduates. Further, the timing of this surge suggests that remote work—not generative AI—explains the bulk of the rise in youth unemployment.
insights.locator: Remote Work Leaves Younger Workers Sidelined

insights.claim: State-level interest rate caps on consumer loans lead riskier borrowers to face credit rationing—but despite taking on less debt, these borrowers did not experience an improvement in delinquencies, and lenders reallocate credit to somewhat safer borrowers instead.
insights.speaker: Rajashri Chakrabarti, Gabriel Leonard, Donald P. Morgan, Thu Pham, Lee Seltzer
insights.so_what: Interest rate caps have the perverse effect of excluding risky borrowers from credit without improving their financial health—relevant to state-level consumer lending regulation debates and to investors assessing alternative credit providers' exposure to cap-related revenue loss.
insights.quote: loan balances for the riskiest borrowers declined substantially relative to counterparts in states without caps. Despite taking on less debt, these borrowers did not experience an improvement in delinquencies.
insights.locator: The Unintended Effects of Interest Rate Caps

insights.claim: Small businesses in the NY/NJ/CT Second District reported severe declines in employment and revenue growth in 2025 and became more pessimistic about 2026, significantly underperforming national small businesses which saw stable revenues and shallower expectation declines.
insights.speaker: Will Aarons, Asani Sarkar
insights.so_what: The NY metro area small business picture is materially worse than national trends—a leading indicator for regional employment and consumer spending that could weigh on NY-area banks' loan quality and local government revenues.
insights.quote: regional small businesses reported severe declines in employment and revenue growth in 2025 and became more pessimistic about growth in 2026
